summary: Matt Rocklin explains his decision to intentionally cut Coiled's headcount from 40 to 20 people, arguing that smaller teams move faster. He identifies a common startup failure pattern where companies raise large rounds, over-hire, and then struggle. Drawing from his experience, he found that adding more people increased organizational inertia and created integration burdens rather than accelerating progress. The company is now back to a lean team with years of runway and a focus on building rather than meetings.
key_points: Many startups over-raised during the 2021 funding boom and hired prematurely, setting themselves up for future contraction
Adding more smart people didn't make the company faster—it increased inertia and created organizational overhead
Early-stage startups need to pivot rapidly, which becomes nearly impossible as team size grows
Coiled proactively contracted before financial stress forced the decision, preserving a multi-year runway
The company now prioritizes culture fit and specific skill needs over headcount growth
money_quotes: I've intentionally shrunk Coiled from 40 to 20 people. This was painful, but we're moving faster as a result.
We hired lots of smart people thinking that more smart people would make us go faster. This wasn't my experience.
Adding more smart people was bad in two ways: Increased inertia making it harder to perform the rapid pivoting that's necessary for a young company.

When we walk down a street with a single friend we walk quickly, even if we need to navigate around an obstacle like a construction site. When we walk down the same street with a gang of many friends we're slow.
Early stage startups are like navigating an entire city under construction, everyone needs to be able to pivot together at a moment's notice.
This isn't our story, but it would have been given time.
My calendar is clear of meetings, the team is aligned, and I am glad to be back to thinking about users, technology, and communication.
key_insight: For early-stage startups, organizational agility matters more than headcount—adding people creates coordination overhead that can slow you down more than their contributions speed you up.
